title: Emotional Baggage

jokes.text: Michael accidentally bails out the family attorney instead of Buster
jokes.setup: Michael is desperately trying to get his brother out of jail
jokes.punchline: He accidentally bails out the incompetent family lawyer instead
jokes.explanation: A perfect encapsulation of the Bluth family's perpetual incompetence spiral. Works because it's entirely believable given both Michael's flustered state and Barry's uselessness. The irony that the lawyer benefits from Michael's mistake is richly character-specific.

jokes.text: Barry asking 'Couldn't you embe... Borrow from your company?' and then suggesting George Michael's company
jokes.setup: Barry is supposed to be Michael's lawyer and advisor
jokes.punchline: He nearly says 'embezzle' twice in succession, barely catching himself, suggesting embezzlement as casually as borrowing a cup of sugar
jokes.explanation: The repeated self-correction 'embe... Borrow' is a masterclass in character comedy — Barry is so comfortable with financial crime that embezzlement is his default suggestion, and he can barely remember the legal word. The repetition of the bit with George Michael's company elevates it further. Earns a high score for character integration.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Michael had been looking for an opportunity to come clean about Fakeblock... But this wasn't it.'
jokes.setup: George Michael says Fakeblock money is 'tied up in legalities, patenting, marketing'
jokes.punchline: Narrator flatly notes he was looking for a chance to confess it's fake — but declined this one too
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan deflation is perfectly economical. The juxtaposition of Michael's encouraging 'Sounds amazing' with the narrator's truth-telling is a hallmark AD structural move. Strong economy score.

jokes.text: Barry: 'You don't have the courtesy to wait until I'm disbarred?'
jokes.setup: Michael threatens to use another lawyer
jokes.punchline: Barry's wounded response implies disbarment is an inevitability he's simply waiting to occur — treating it as a scheduling conflict
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The joke treats Barry's disbarment as a fait accompli he's emotionally prepared for — the only offense is Michael's rudeness in not waiting. It's perfectly constructed, deeply character-specific (only Barry could say this), and highly quotable. Economy is exceptional: maximum information per word. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Barry: 'I was gonna use your divorce to pay off my peekaboo lawyer.'
jokes.setup: Barry explains his financial planning
jokes.punchline: He casually reveals he has a 'peekaboo lawyer' — an absurd job title delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'peekaboo lawyer' is wonderfully bizarre and the fact that Barry says it without explanation or apology is perfectly in character. AD's economy of absurdity at work.

jokes.text: Michael: 'What part of Mom is the fancy?' and 'Oh, God, the thought of your feet.'
jokes.setup: Lucille describes walking on the beach with her new man and liking 'the feel of sand on our feet'
jokes.punchline: Gob's visceral horror at the thought of his mother's feet — a deliberately small, specific, and therefore funnier detail to fixate on
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Gob's horror lands on 'feet' rather than anything more overtly sexual — it's the specificity of the mundane detail that makes it funnier than going for an obvious target.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm glad you have someone to talk to.' [then immediately] 'Well, maybe I'll go check on Buster.'
jokes.setup: The increasingly uncomfortable conversation about Lucille's romantic life
jokes.punchline: Michael's sudden pivot — unconvincingly claiming gladness before fleeing the conversation entirely
jokes.explanation: A solid Michael beat — his repression and inability to process family dysfunction expressed as polite retreat. Functional but not especially memorable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Mom really has him fancy-whipped, huh?'
jokes.setup: Gob caves immediately to Lucille's preferences and exits
jokes.punchline: Michael coins 'fancy-whipped' — a portmanteau of 'hen-pecked/whipped' and the earlier 'tickles my fancy' euphemism
jokes.explanation: A tidy callback that creates a new compound phrase. Works well as a button on the scene. The economy and wordplay are solid.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Yeah, Lottie Dottie DA, my asshole.'
jokes.setup: Barry dramatically says he hates to even say the DA's name
jokes.punchline: Her name turns out to be a reference to the Slick Rick/Doug E. Fresh rap lyric 'Lottie Dottie' — but Barry uses it as a dismissal/expletive as if it's an insult
jokes.explanation: The joke subverts the dramatic build-up with an absurd non-name. Barry's casual vulgarity as punctuation is in character. Works better if you catch the rap reference.

jokes.text: Barry asking whether the press blackout covers 'scripted' shows as well as reality — 'Why would there be a scripted about the family?'
jokes.setup: Barry agrees to a total press blackout — then asks if that covers scripted TV
jokes.punchline: Michael's confused 'Why would there be a scripted about the family?' is itself the meta-joke — they ARE in a scripted show about the family
jokes.explanation: AD's meta-textual comedy is at its best when characters are completely unaware of the irony. Michael's genuine bafflement makes this land. Rewatch bonus because you catch the layers better on second viewing.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Weren't you working on one with Ron Howard?' — the Ron Howard docuseries meta-reveal
jokes.setup: The question of whether a scripted show about the Bluths could exist
jokes.punchline: Barry casually references the Ron Howard project that IS the show the audience is watching
jokes.explanation: AD's foundational meta-joke — Ron Howard narrates the show while being a character in it. The 'grandfathered in... before the murder' addition gives it an extra twist. Rewatch bonus for catching the full implication.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Can't. Kitty hates me.' / Gob: 'First, you eat it with Buster, then you step in it with the movie, and now you're eating what you stepped in, because Kitty hates you.'
jokes.setup: Michael keeps compounding his problems
jokes.punchline: Gob delivers an increasingly tortured food/disgust metaphor that ends in the logically absurd 'eating what you stepped in'
jokes.explanation: Gob's gleeful breakdown of Michael's cascading failures is perfectly in character — Gob loves watching Michael suffer. The escalating mixed metaphor 'eating what you stepped in' is a strong structural joke that earns its punchline through committed escalation.

jokes.text: 'Geo-bead' — Gob uses one of George Sr.'s made-up expressions meaning 'screw-up'
jokes.setup: Gob calls Michael a 'geo-bead' — a term no one has heard before
jokes.punchline: It's one of George Sr.'s made-up insult expressions that only Gob knows, treated as completely legitimate vocabulary
jokes.explanation: A small but excellently crafted joke. 'Geo-bead' sounds just plausible enough as a regional/family expression while being completely made-up. The explanation that it's one of 'Dad's expressions... you'll figure it out someday' adds layers about the Bluth family's insularity and George Sr.'s cryptic dominance. Highly quotable precisely because it means nothing.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Would that be helpful if I did? Or am I just being a G-O-B?' / Michael's pause / Gob: 'G-O-B spells Gob, you geo-bead'
jokes.setup: Gob offers to help and asks if he's 'being a G-O-B' — implying it's a negative quality
jokes.punchline: He spells out his own name as if it's an insult, then catches himself — realizing mid-sentence that insulting himself with his own name is absurd
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed moment. Gob uses his own name as a pejorative insult ('Am I just being a G-O-B?'), then has to explain 'G-O-B spells Gob' — meaning he just called himself an idiot with his own name, and has to correct Michael for not catching his self-deprecating insult. The Arrested Development DNA is all over this: dense, fast, character-specific, and it doubles back on the geo-bead bit. Near-exceptional character integration — only Gob could stumble into this particular trap.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Would that be helpful if I did? Or am I just being a G-O-B?' — then realizing: 'G-O-B spells Gob, you geo-bead, so why don't you s... ah. Well, today's the day.'
jokes.setup: Gob offers to help, then tries to use his own initials as a self-deprecating joke
jokes.punchline: Gob realizes mid-sentence that spelling out G-O-B just spells his name, destroying his own point — and then apparently has a mental realization so significant he calls it 'today's the day'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak for Gob. The construction is multilayered: Gob uses 'geo-bead' correctly as an insult, then catches himself mid-insult when his self-defense collapses (because G-O-B does just spell Gob), and then has some private epiphany so large that today becomes 'the day.' It's perfect Gob — simultaneously insult, self-own, and existential revelation in the same breath. Justified 8+ across the board for character integration and structure.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Why are you even still here? Go find your wife.' / Gob: 'I don't know where she is.'
jokes.setup: Michael dismisses Gob to deal with his own problems
jokes.punchline: Gob's flat admission that he has no idea where his wife is — delivered without distress, just as a logistical fact
jokes.explanation: Functional joke. The deadpan delivery of 'I don't know where she is' as a casual shrug rather than a crisis is Gob-specific, but it's not particularly novel for the character or the show.

jokes.text: Maeby to George Michael: 'Why don't we make out again and teach them a lesson?'
jokes.setup: Maeby and George Michael reunite at a family party
jokes.punchline: Maeby casually suggests cousin-kissing as a social power move — the 'lesson' framing makes it even more disturbing
jokes.explanation: The casual suggestion of incest as a tactical maneuver is peak Maeby — she weaponizes everything, including her own cousin. The 'teach them a lesson' rationale adds an extra layer of absurdist logic.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I can't, 'cause my dad's here, and he thinks I'm still dating Rebel.' / Maeby: 'And how would our making out teach them a lesson?' / George Michael: 'T-Teach Rebel a lesson. I meant, teach Rebel.'
jokes.setup: George Michael objects to making out because of his dad — not because they're cousins
jokes.punchline: He scrambles to clarify the 'lesson' was for Rebel, not family — but his objection was the dad, not the incest, which he seemed fine with
jokes.explanation: The joke is in what George Michael DOESN'T say — his objection is his dad being present, not the fact that it's his cousin. This is a layered, dark-subversive observation about how normalized the Maeby dynamic has become for him. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: George Michael's plan: ghost Rebel for two months, show up 'demanding sex,' she breaks up with him — but she was out of town and now thinks they're closer than ever
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby had a plan for him to exit his relationship with Rebel
jokes.punchline: Through pure coincidence and inaction, George Michael's plan to be a jerk made him look devoted — she thinks they're 'closer than ever'
jokes.explanation: A brilliant comic irony that is deeply structural to AD's comedic DNA: the Bluth incompetence that makes things worse accidentally makes things better in the worst possible way. George Michael doing nothing looked like patient devotion. The 'demanding sex after ghosting' plan being foiled by her simply being away is an exceptional construction. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Maeby asking if George Michael is jealous of Murphybrown — George Michael saying 'I mean, the guy grew up not even knowing his father. If anything, I'm... jealous.' Then Maeby: 'Yeah, that... that is a great word for it.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is asked if he's jealous of Tobias's new found son Murphybrown
jokes.punchline: George Michael says 'I'm jealous' about someone who grew up without a father — when his own absent father situation is the defining trauma of the show — and Maeby's 'yeah, that's a great word for it' doubles the irony
jokes.explanation: Maeby's 'that is a great word for it' knowing acknowledgment of the unspoken irony is a nice layered beat. Works through implication rather than statement.

jokes.text: Michael overhearing that Rebel doesn't want George Michael to meet her family — and then Michael's 'I failed the Ron test big-time' confession
jokes.setup: Michael wades into advice about George Michael's relationship with Rebel
jokes.punchline: He reveals he himself 'failed the Ron test' when he was 'briefly interested' in Rebel — the father and son have been dating the same woman
jokes.explanation: The running joke of Michael and George Michael unknowingly competing for the same woman gets another excellent turn here. Michael's careful downplaying of his interest ('even that overstates it') while clearly having real feelings is great character work.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I also thought that he was... Her... Her lover? / George Michael: Boss.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains he failed the 'Ron test' when briefly interested in Rebel
jokes.punchline: Michael thought Ron Howard was Rebel's lover — he was actually her boss/father — revealing Michael's paranoid romantic imagination
jokes.explanation: The escalating pause before 'lover' — and George Michael's flat correction 'Boss' — is a nice economy-of-dialogue joke. Michael's romantic delusion about Ron Howard is a funny character beat.

jokes.text: Michael: 'No. No, well, I had a great role model.' / Ron: 'Aw, bless you.' / Michael: 'Yeah, I also had a...' / Ron: 'Well, bless you.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he had a great role model for not being a sycophant
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard takes the compliment before Michael finishes — then does it again when Michael starts a second sentence — Ron is so accustomed to being complimented he hears it preemptively
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured two-beat joke. Ron Howard preemptively accepting a compliment that wasn't directed at him — and then doing it again — is a precision-built character joke. The repetition is perfectly calibrated: one more 'bless you' would be too much, one less would lose the pattern. Ron Howard playing a self-absorbed version of himself is great meta-comedy.

jokes.text: Ron's advice: 'If you end up meeting that family, one good rule to follow is — do not bring Tobias.' / George Michael: 'Be myself.'
jokes.setup: Ron gives George Michael earnest relationship advice and starts a rule
jokes.punchline: George Michael completes Ron's sentence with 'be myself' — Ron was actually going to say 'don't bring Tobias' — the gap between cliché and reality is the joke
jokes.explanation: The setup lures you into expecting 'be yourself' — the clichéd advice punchline — and Ron Howard's actual punchline ('don't bring Tobias') is both funnier and more specific. Perfect misdirection structure.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'Yes, be George Maharis, George Michael.'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard gives advice about being authentic
jokes.punchline: He tells George Michael to 'be George Maharis' — the fake name — completely undermining the 'be yourself' advice he just echoed
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: Ron endorsing George Michael's fake identity, the irony of 'be yourself' advice delivered to someone living a lie, and the rhythm of 'be George Maharis, George Michael.' Good economy.

jokes.text: Michael's confused internal monologue: 'I had to give her Buster. The movie couldn't be me and George Michael. We were both dating Rebel, and she didn't know that he was my son.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains his movie strategy
jokes.punchline: The explanation reveals the full absurdity of the situation — father and son competing for the same woman who doesn't know they're related
jokes.explanation: Functional recap-joke that lays out the absurdity without adding much new comedy. The situation is inherently funny but this articulation of it is more expository than comedic.

jokes.text: Gob's confession: 'Who were you dating?' / Michael: 'Julie Bowen!' — and the narrator reveals it was actually Tony Wonder
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael discuss who they were each dating during the Rebel confusion
jokes.punchline: Michael claims he was dating Julie Bowen (actress) — narrator reveals it was actually Tony Wonder, the gay magician Gob is in love with
jokes.explanation: A masterfully layered joke. Michael deflects with 'Julie Bowen' (a real actress, adding meta absurdity) but the narrator reveals the truth — he was dating Tony Wonder, the man Gob is obsessively in love with. The revelation that Michael dated the object of Gob's unrequited feelings — without knowing it — is a perfect AD cross-character joke. The narrator as truth-teller puncturing Michael's lie is excellent structure.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Tony Wonder, a man Gob had been longing to hear from ever since the gay magician poofed into his life, and then, just as quickly... Disa-poofed.'
jokes.setup: Setting up Tony Wonder's history with Gob
jokes.punchline: 'Disa-poofed' — a portmanteau of 'disappeared' and 'poofed' (British slang/slur for gay) — delivered with complete narrator deadpan
jokes.explanation: A compact, brilliant wordplay invention. 'Disa-poofed' earns its high economy score — it conveys both the disappearing act metaphor and the gay-coded language in a single invented word. Very quotable. AD at its wordplay best.

jokes.text: Michael and Gob discussing whether it 'never made sense' that Rebel liked George Michael — each talking about themselves
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob discuss Rebel losing interest in George Michael
jokes.punchline: Both Michael and an unnamed party are revealed to be talking about themselves — 'it never made sense' that Rebel preferred George Michael over Michael, Gob's eager agreement reveals his complete obliviousness
jokes.explanation: The joke works through dramatic irony — the audience knows Michael is talking about himself, and his self-pitying monologue 'He's gonna need a father... Still needs a father' is a darkly funny self-rationalization.

jokes.text: Michael and Gob: 'The only mother figure he's ever had in his life was... Oh, it was Mom.' / 'Yeah... Huh, poor kid.' / 'I can't imagine.' / Pause / 'Wait, we had Mom.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob lament that George Michael lacked a mother figure
jokes.punchline: They take a full beat to realize they are also describing their own childhood — they had the same mother — 'Wait, we had Mom'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed slow-burn joke. Michael and Gob spend several lines feeling sorry for George Michael's lack of a maternal figure before realizing — simultaneously, with genuine surprise — that they share the same terrible mother. The delayed comprehension, the shared 'I can't imagine,' and the final 'Wait, we had Mom' are perfectly timed. Deeply character-specific and earns every beat. Near-exceptional.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'In a way, she was our mom.' / 'George Michael was seeking motherly advice from a cousin who'd been living as a grandma.'
jokes.setup: The brothers reflect on Lucille being their mother
jokes.punchline: Cut to George Michael seeking 'motherly advice' from Maeby, who has been posing as an elderly woman
jokes.explanation: The transition from 'she was our mom' to George Michael seeking motherly advice from a fake grandma is a nice structural irony. The narrator's deadpan framing elevates what could be a simple scene transition.

jokes.text: Maeby (as elderly Annette) offers George Michael a lick of something — he recoils — she says 'Good, more for me'
jokes.setup: Maeby in her old-woman disguise greets her cousin
jokes.punchline: She offers him a lick of something unidentifiable with complete sincerity — the joke is her full commitment to a disgusting old-person offer
jokes.explanation: A solid visual gag — the unidentified lick-able item and Maeby's elderly character committing to the gross-out offer works well. Functional cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Stan: 'If your grandson wasn't here, I'd probably take a peeky-weeky.'
jokes.setup: Maeby (as elderly Annette) says she has no makeup on
jokes.punchline: Stan uses the baby-talk phrase 'peeky-weeky' while flirting with an elderly woman — the infantilized language makes the flirtation more disturbing
jokes.explanation: 'Peeky-weeky' is an unexpectedly funny word choice that makes the flirtation more cringe-worthy. The joke works on the absurdist creepiness of Stan's apparent interest in the 'elderly' Annette.

jokes.text: George Michael's reaction to Maeby living with Stan: 'You're living with this guy?' / Maeby: 'Hey, my fake identity is none of your fake identity's business, okay?'
jokes.setup: George Michael is surprised to find Maeby living with an older man
jokes.punchline: Maeby's 'my fake identity is none of your fake identity's business' — both cousins are living under false identities and she weaponizes the mutual lie for privacy
jokes.explanation: An exceptional line. 'My fake identity is none of your fake identity's business' is both supremely quotable and deeply specific to this show — it could only be said between two characters both living under false identities. The parallelism of the phrase is elegant and the logic is airtight in its absurdity. High quotability.

jokes.text: Maeby's plan: show up to the Howard barbecue with pink hair to repel Ron Howard — 'I saw it on someone recently, and it made me want to puke. Trust me.'
jokes.setup: Maeby advises George Michael on how to sabotage his relationship with Rebel
jokes.punchline: The pink hair she suggests was seen on Tobias — who currently has pink hair — meaning she's suggesting George Michael get Tobias's exact hair, which she found repulsive
jokes.explanation: The callback to Tobias's pink hair is well-planted — Maeby doesn't know she's recommending her own father's look. A dense AD callback that rewards attention.

jokes.text: George Michael's hair dye session: 'That's starting to tingle.' / Maeby: 'Let me know when it starts to burn.' / George Michael: 'It's gonna burn?' / Maeby: 'Did I not make that clear?' / 'Nobody said getting out of a relationship would be painless.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is applying hair dye to George Michael's head
jokes.punchline: She casually reveals it will burn — she knew the whole time — and philosophizes 'nobody said getting out of a relationship would be painless' while scalding her cousin
jokes.explanation: The escalating dye-burn sequence works well. Maeby's 'Did I not make that clear?' and the relationship metaphor button are both strong. The philosophical pivot at the end is characteristically Maeby — weaponizing clichés while literally causing pain.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'You should talk about getting out of a relationship. I think your friend Stan might be interested in you in a way that you don't want him to be interested.'
jokes.setup: Maeby philosophizes about relationship pain
jokes.punchline: George Michael turns it around — pointing out Maeby's own entangled fake-relationship situation, setting up the episode's eventual reveal that Stan's interest is paternal
jokes.explanation: Works as setup for the paternal reveal payoff later in the episode. Not especially funny on its own but earns rewatch bonus for foreshadowing.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'That would explain why he's offering to do my laundry... Well, whatever gets him to do my laundry, huh?'
jokes.setup: George Michael suggests Stan might be romantically interested in Maeby
jokes.punchline: Maeby's reaction is not horror but pragmatic satisfaction — 'whatever gets him to do my laundry' — she'll exploit his romantic interest for domestic services
jokes.explanation: A great Maeby character beat. Her immediate pivot to exploitation is perfectly in character — Maeby has always been the most mercenary Bluth-adjacent character. 'Whatever gets him to do my laundry' is a good button.

jokes.text: Tobias about his TV predator role: 'Technically, they had to call me Rock Monster... John Beard actually said I was one of the more popular predators, so whatever gets your name and weight out there.'
jokes.setup: Tobias proudly discusses his TV career as a 'predator' on a catch-predator show
jokes.punchline: He's proud of being a 'popular predator' and frames it as career advancement — the dark implication of being a literal predator is played completely straight by Tobias
jokes.explanation: Tobias's complete obliviousness to the implication of being proud of being a 'popular predator' is very on-brand. 'Whatever gets your name and weight out there' is a great touch — he treats predator notoriety like a showbiz credit.

jokes.text: Tobias to Murphybrown: 'I didn't ask, "What part of a dog do you want to touch?"'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to help Murphybrown discover his passion/dreams
jokes.punchline: Murphybrown mentions loving animals and connecting with them — Tobias misinterprets the conversation into the most alarming possible paraphrase
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias — his well-meaning coaching accidentally produces the most inappropriate possible version of what someone just said. 'What part of a dog do you want to touch?' is a line that shouldn't be funny but is, delivered from a character whose obliviousness makes everything sound wrong.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Let's try this in reverse. You're famous. What is the thing you do that makes everyone clap?' / Murphybrown: 'Ooh! Once I got claps for doing a juggling ball thing, 'cause I'd unicycle in Haiti when I was volunteering...' / Tobias: 'Yes! Clowning, yes! Now, you're dreaming.'
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to coach Murphybrown to discover his passion
jokes.punchline: Tobias seizes on 'unicycling in Haiti' and 'juggling' as evidence of Murphybrown's clowning destiny — completely ignoring the humanitarian context
jokes.explanation: Tobias's theatrical coaching style, combined with his total disregard for the humanitarian context of volunteering in Haiti, is excellent character comedy. 'Now you're dreaming' as a triumphant declaration about clowning is very Tobias.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Well, perhaps we should juggle your priorities.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just identified Murphybrown's 'passion' as juggling/clowning
jokes.punchline: 'Perhaps we should juggle your priorities' — a pun on 'juggle' that references both the literal juggling they just discussed and the idiom 'juggle priorities'
jokes.explanation: A clean, well-timed pun that earns its placement by being directly tied to the scene's action. Tobias's tendency to deploy puns in earnest is a character trait that makes these land differently than if another character said them.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I believe in you. You can do her.' / Gob's confused look / Michael: 'This. You can do this... to her.'
jokes.setup: Michael encourages Gob before approaching Kitty
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'you can do her' — clearly meaning 'handle this' — realizes the double entendre, and tries to correct it while making it worse with 'to her'
jokes.explanation: A reliable double-entendre joke elevated by Michael's frantic correction that makes it worse. 'You can do this... to her' is a great example of a character digging deeper into a hole while trying to climb out.

jokes.text: Kitty to Gob: 'Well, aren't you a sight for sore eyes.' / Gob: 'I was gonna say, they look puffy and red.'
jokes.setup: Kitty greets Gob warmly with 'a sight for sore eyes'
jokes.punchline: Gob takes the idiom literally and offers a medical observation about her eyes being puffy and red
jokes.explanation: A perfectly economical joke. Gob's literal reading of an idiom is character-specific (he's too self-absorbed to understand social warmth) and the medical specificity 'puffy and red' is the ideal punchline. Clean construction.

jokes.text: Michael suggesting they 'get a room' so he can 'say hi to Ron' — and Kitty revealing Ron isn't in the office because of 'some family event'
jokes.setup: Michael makes an excuse to separate from Gob and Kitty's reunion
jokes.punchline: Ron's 'family event' will turn out to be the Howard barbecue where George Michael is currently showing up with red hair — a dramatic irony the audience suspects but Michael doesn't
jokes.explanation: Works primarily as structural setup for the convergence of storylines. Earns rewatch bonus for catching the connection.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'How would you like to get inside of a LEM?' / Gob: 'If I could j... Um, Michael... I just... I can't do this.'
jokes.setup: Kitty invites Gob into a Lunar Excursion Module at the film company
jokes.punchline: Gob's flustered 'I can't do this' — the LEM invitation sounds sexual, Gob nearly says something, then panics at the prospect of being alone with Kitty in an enclosed space
jokes.explanation: The sexual ambiguity of 'get inside of a LEM' sets up Gob's panic well. His near-sentence 'If I could j...' suggests what he almost said. Good cringe setup for what follows.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You can't make me touch her hair.' / Michael: 'I'm not gonna touch her hair.' — Michael coaching Gob by reassuring himself
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob are both psyching themselves up to deal with Kitty
jokes.punchline: Michael's reassurance 'I'm not gonna touch her hair' is clearly also for himself — both brothers are equally reluctant and squeamish about Kitty
jokes.explanation: Michael ostensibly coaching Gob but actually reassuring himself is a nice character beat. The specificity of 'touch her hair' as the line Michael draws is funny.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'George Maharis was ready to alienate his soon-to-be ex-girlfriend's family with a head full of Tobias Pink hair.'
jokes.setup: Maeby's plan to give George Michael pink hair to repel Ron Howard
jokes.punchline: The narrator calls it 'Tobias Pink' hair — connecting George Michael's new look directly to his uncle — foreshadowing the hair will match the family instead of repelling them
jokes.explanation: The narrator's precise labeling 'Tobias Pink' is a nice anticipatory joke. The audience knows what's coming — the hair won't be repulsive but rather endearing to the Howards.

jokes.text: George Michael's hair turns out to be 'Howard Red' — matching Ron Howard's family hair color exactly
jokes.setup: Maeby's plan was to make George Michael's hair garish pink to repel Ron Howard
jokes.punchline: The dye left in too long turns the hair not pink but 'Howard Red' — the exact shade of the Howard family's red hair — making him look like he's courting the family rather than repelling them
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect comedic reversal. The plan to repel through pink hair becomes the opposite — Howard family red — making George Michael look like he's desperately trying to fit in. The layers compound: Maeby suggesting Tobias's pink hair, the dye reacting to produce Howard Red, and the family's delighted recognition ('Someone's come a-courtin'!') — each layer earned. Structure is exceptional: setup planted early, payoff lands on multiple levels simultaneously. One of the episode's best constructed jokes.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'It's Howard Red. Cheryl, look at what this kid did. It's like our hair. Someone's come a-courtin'.'
jokes.setup: George Michael's hair is accidentally Howard Red
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard interprets it as intentional courtship — 'someone's come a-courtin'' — treating an accident as a charming romantic gesture
jokes.explanation: Ron's delight at reading the accidental hair match as intentional courting is a great dramatic-irony beat. 'Someone's come a-courtin'' is a charming and funny line from Ron Howard, playing a version of himself who is wholesome to the point of anachronism.

jokes.text: Rebel: 'We Howards don't just laugh when things are funny. We laugh when we're happy. And when things are uncomfortable, too.' / George Michael: 'You mean, when bad reviews start rolling in?'
jokes.setup: Rebel explains the Howard family's laughter habits
jokes.punchline: George Michael's immediate pivot to 'bad reviews' — a very specific Hollywood insult aimed at a Hollywood dynasty — completely destroys the sweet family moment
jokes.explanation: George Michael's reflexive cynicism puncturing the Howard family's warm self-mythology is sharply funny. 'Bad reviews start rolling in' is the exact wrong thing to say and the specificity makes it land hard. Good economy.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'The company's gonna go under by next week. We're broke.' followed immediately by 'Guess I'm not exactly daddy's dream boyfriend, am I?'
jokes.setup: Rebel is enthusiastically pitching Fakeblock to Ron Howard
jokes.punchline: George Michael blurts the actual truth — 'We're broke' — then immediately tries to frame it as self-deprecating charm
jokes.explanation: George Michael's compulsive truth-telling about Fakeblock is a good character beat. The quick pivot to self-deprecation is endearing and the joke works as escalation of his truth-blurting tendency.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'And in those early days, I can't tell you how many times I felt like a fraud. But I'll tell you a secret, everyone feels that way.'
jokes.setup: Ron gives George Michael sincere advice about feeling like a fraud in business
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard, a legitimately successful filmmaker, tells George Michael — who runs an ACTUALLY fake company — that everyone feels like a fraud. George Michael IS a fraud. The advice is accidentally exactly right for the wrong reason.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dramatic irony. Ron Howard's sincere reassurance that 'everyone feels like a fraud' lands perfectly because George Michael is a LITERAL fraud running a fake company. The advice is both completely wrong in context and accidentally correct in a way that makes it worse. This is AD's signature joke structure: sincerity colliding with layers of deception. High earned vs. cheap score because it works entirely through character and plot logic.

jokes.text: George Michael's attempt to alienate Ron Howard by saying 'No, I don't like hot dogs' — followed by 'I love them.' — then everyone's delight
jokes.setup: George Michael has made one last attempt to offend his host, saying he doesn't like hot dogs
jokes.punchline: He immediately contradicts himself — 'I love them' — completely unable to actually be dislikeable to this warm family
jokes.explanation: The setup-and-immediate-contradiction is a perfect joke structure. George Michael's inability to maintain a lie when faced with actual hot dogs is deeply character-specific — he's too honest, too nice, to actually execute Maeby's plan. The family's delight ('No. Oh, my God. You're good.') makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Meanwhile, Gob was trying to get in the mood with Kitty.' / Visual: Gob in the space capsule LEM
jokes.setup: Scene transition
jokes.punchline: The narrator's utterly flat 'trying to get in the mood with Kitty' followed by the absurd setting — a replica space capsule — as a seduction location
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan delivery of Gob's ludicrous situation is a reliable AD device. Works well here.

jokes.text: Gob and Kitty in the LEM: 'Hard to believe they used to put monkeys in these.' / Kitty: 'My guess? They wouldn't be able to figure out a lot of this stuff.' / Gob: 'Yeah. Monkeys are kind of dumb. I'd like to see one get out of here quickly in an emergency. They'd be all...' [performs monkey impression] 'I have to get out of here. Get me out of here.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Kitty are sealed inside a Lunar Excursion Module
jokes.punchline: Gob's monkey impression while trapped in the LEM perfectly mirrors his own panicked behavior — he is the monkey who can't figure out how to get out in an emergency
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed joke. Gob performs an impression of a panicking monkey trapped in a capsule — immediately before panicking and being unable to escape the capsule himself. The self-referential irony is embedded in the joke's structure without being pointed out. 'Ah, too good, too good, too good' from presumably the narrator confirms the writer's own delight. The physical comedy of Gob enacting the very situation he's about to be in is exceptional. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Gob seeing Michael's text 'Did you trick her into it yet?' and responding 'Trick her into what?'
jokes.setup: Gob checks his phone during his alone time with Kitty
jokes.punchline: Michael's text reveals Gob was supposed to trick Kitty into giving back movie rights — Gob has completely forgotten the mission
jokes.explanation: Gob's total amnesia about his actual mission is in character. Functional comedy but not especially novel.

jokes.text: Tony Wonder calling from inside a Tumi suitcase in baggage claim — 'My assistant's picking me up, unless she takes another gray Tumi by mistake, then I go to unclaimed. That's how we lost the Amazing Frederick.'
jokes.setup: Gob asks where Tony Wonder is
jokes.punchline: Tony is calling from inside a suitcase in baggage claim — and this is apparently such a common occurrence that there's a precedent: 'That's how we lost the Amazing Frederick'
jokes.explanation: An exceptional piece of absurdist character comedy. The premise — Tony Wonder calling from inside a Tumi suitcase in baggage claim — is wonderfully specific. But 'That's how we lost the Amazing Frederick' takes it to another level: there is apparently a HISTORY of this, an entire named magician lost to baggage claim. This is the kind of off-hand joke that defines AD's comedic density. Every word earns its place. Highly quotable and creates a vivid throwaway character in five words.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I would've claimed you.' / Tony: 'I'll bet.'
jokes.setup: Tony explains he might end up in unclaimed baggage
jokes.punchline: Gob's earnest 'I would've claimed you' is a love declaration disguised as practical reassurance — Tony's 'I'll bet' is knowing acknowledgment of Gob's feelings
jokes.explanation: Four words that contain an entire romantic storyline. 'I would've claimed you' is Gob's most sincere expression of his feelings for Tony Wonder in the episode, dressed as a practical logistics statement. Tony's 'I'll bet' acknowledges both the practicality and the subtext simultaneously. Economy score near-perfect. Extremely quotable in isolation.

jokes.text: Tony: 'I'm the flamboyant gay magician. You're the super straight Christian magician. We'll taint each other's brand.'
jokes.setup: Tony explains why they can't hang out
jokes.punchline: 'We'll taint each other's brand' — the word 'taint' doing double duty, the irony that Gob's 'super straight' brand is the very thing he's questioning, and the absurdity of magicians having 'brands' they protect
jokes.explanation: The layered irony of Gob — who is clearly not 'super straight Christian' — being kept at arm's length by Tony because of his 'brand' is richly character-specific. The word 'taint' is doing a lot of work. Rewatch bonus for catching how much it says about Gob's self-image.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I guess we're... different in the same way.' / Tony: 'Same, in a different way.' / [deteriorating into] 'Different, wifferent... In a same gay way? Wame.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Tony try to articulate their complicated relationship with words
jokes.punchline: Their philosophical attempt to describe their dynamic devolves from 'different in the same way' through multiple iterations into 'wifferent,' 'same gay way,' and finally 'Wame' — the conversation collapses under its own semantic weight
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout moments. The joke begins coherently and escalates through precise linguistic disintegration — each iteration makes grammatical sense as a response to the last, but moves further from meaning. 'Wifferent,' 'same gay way' (which accidentally blurts the truth), and the final collapsed portmanteau 'Wame' represent a perfectly structured linguistic unraveling. The accidental 'same gay way' is a Freudian slip the show lets hang there. Exceptional structure and character integration — only Gob and Tony's relationship could produce this specific kind of beautiful nonsense.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I better shut up and listen for "wonder."' [pause] 'Wait, so is this goodbye...'
jokes.setup: Gob says he should stop talking and listen for Tony's next word
jokes.punchline: 'Listen for wonder' — both a magical instruction (listen for a wonder/miracle) and a literal instruction to listen for Tony Wonder's name — immediately undercut by Gob failing to listen
jokes.explanation: A compact wordplay joke that works on two levels simultaneously. Gob immediately talking after saying he'll listen is a good self-undermining character beat.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'How much did you hear?'
jokes.setup: Gob has been having an intimate phone conversation with Tony Wonder while sealed in the LEM with Kitty
jokes.punchline: Kitty's 'How much did you hear?' — she has heard everything, including the 'same gay way' slip, and the question itself confirms her suspicion while being the only appropriate reaction
jokes.explanation: Kitty's four-word reaction is perfectly economical. The irony is complete — she was there the whole time, so of course she heard everything. The question implies she's processing what she heard rather than oblivious to it. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: George Sr. at prison: 'This is where I ate a battery during my first week at recess. No, no, "yard." Don't call it recess.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is touring the prison where Buster is now incarcerated
jokes.punchline: He casually mentions eating a battery as a prison initiation — and his only correction is the terminology 'yard' vs 'recess,' not the battery consumption
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect economy joke. George Sr. treats eating a battery as a mundane prison memory — the only thing he's concerned about correcting is the word 'recess.' The hierarchy of concerns is perfectly inverted. 'Don't call it recess' as the corrective note, rather than 'don't eat batteries,' is a genuinely great piece of character comedy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Prison guard recognizing George Sr.: 'I've never seen you in street clothes.' / George Sr.: 'Duck?' / Guard: 'I almost didn't recognize you without the flashlight in your mouth.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. meets a prison guard he apparently knows
jokes.punchline: 'I almost didn't recognize you without the flashlight in your mouth' — suggests George Sr.'s prison activities were specific and memorable enough to be his identifier
jokes.explanation: The flashlight detail is specific enough to suggest a story the show wisely doesn't tell. George Sr.'s confused 'Duck?' implies a nickname, adding another layer. The guard's warmth makes the dark implication funnier.

jokes.text: George Sr. gives Buster prison survival advice: 'Skinhead gets in your face, you charge back at him. You pop him fast.' / Buster: 'That one there's been giving me looks.' / George Sr.: 'No. That's not a skinhead. That's a guard. You charge at a guard, they'll kill you.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. gives earnest prison wisdom about confronting skinheads
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately identifies the wrong person — a guard — as his target, confirming he will apply the advice in the worst possible way
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured setup/punchline. The setup (George Sr.'s advice) is planted sincerely, and Buster's immediate misidentification of a guard as a skinhead is perfectly Buster — he has always been catastrophically unable to read any social situation. 'You charge at a guard, they'll kill you' as the punchline is an excellent rapid deflation. The joke earns its high structure score because the punchline is earned through character logic, not randomness.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I can't satisfy her.' / Buster: 'Really? It's so easy.' / George Sr.: 'No, I mean, we are no longer together.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. laments his relationship with Lucille
jokes.punchline: His 'I can't satisfy her' is taken sexually by Buster — 'Really? It's so easy' — implying Buster satisfies Lucille, which is an Oedipal horror. George Sr. quickly clarifies it's a separation.
jokes.explanation: The Oedipal horror of Buster's 'Really? It's so easy' is perfectly delivered. Only Buster's particular psychosexual history with Lucille makes this joke possible — it's deeply character-specific. The misdirection setup is clean and the clarification punchline works both to resolve and to deepen the cringe.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'She has found someone new. She's found a younger man. Sort of a son with benefits.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains that Lucille has a new man — a younger one who lives on the beach
jokes.punchline: 'Son with benefits' — a portmanteau/riff on 'friends with benefits' that also precisely describes the Dustin Radler situation AND gestures at the Bluth family's general Oedipal issues
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Son with benefits' is exceptional: it works as a pun on 'friends with benefits,' as an accurate description of Dustin Radler's role (younger man who behaves like a son to Lucille), AND as a dark commentary on the Bluth family's Oedipal dynamics (especially in front of Buster, Lucille's most literally son-husband). Economy is near-perfect — three words that do enormous comedic work. Extremely quotable. Comparable to the highest anchors in the rubric.

jokes.text: Buster: 'They said that I'd lose my old lady, and I said, "Not mother!" "She'll wait for me!"'
jokes.setup: George Sr. reveals Lucille has a new man
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'Not mother!' — referring to Lucille as his 'old lady' and being devastated she won't wait for him — confirms the Oedipal horror while playing it as romantic tragedy
jokes.explanation: Buster referring to Lucille as his 'old lady' and being heartbroken she didn't 'wait for him' is an extremely high character-integration joke — only Buster's precise psychosexual pathology produces this response. 'Not mother!' as his declaration of certainty is particularly funny. The tragedy played straight is better than if it were played for laughs.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'The thought of her singing to him goodnight. I can't take it. I can't take it.' / Buster: 'I know. I know.' — both men united in grief over Lucille
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Buster both express devastation over Lucille's new man
jokes.punchline: Father and son are sharing a genuine moment of grief over the same woman — husband and son both equally devastated — the scene plays completely straight, which is what makes it funny
jokes.explanation: The scene's comedy comes entirely from the grotesqueness of the situation played straight. Father and son bonding over their shared heartbreak over the mother/wife is peak Arrested Development dark comedy.

jokes.text: Prison guard: 'No touching.' — interrupting George Sr. and Buster's emotional moment
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Buster are sharing a genuine emotional moment in prison
jokes.punchline: 'No touching' — the classic AD prison running gag perfectly timed to puncture a sincere emotional beat
jokes.explanation: The 'no touching' gag earns its laugh through perfect timing — interrupting a genuinely poignant father/son moment. Lower originality because it's a callback gag, but perfect economy (two words) and reliable impact from a well-loved running joke.

jokes.text: George Sr. decides to deal with his misery by having someone 'put him out of his misery' — reveals it's just hugging the prison guard 'Duck'
jokes.setup: George Sr. dramatically declares 'I know what I have to do' after lamenting his situation
jokes.punchline: The implication is something drastic — the punchline is he just wanted to hug his prison guard friend 'Duck,' who is exasperated but permits it
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the dramatic build implies something dark, the resolution is George Sr. needing a hug. 'Jesus Christ, George' from Duck is perfect. The relationship between George Sr. and Duck as established figures makes this land.

jokes.text: George Michael arrives at Howard barbecue: 'Your dad is so nice.' / Rebel: 'It's been his job since he was two.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is charmed by Ron Howard
jokes.punchline: Rebel's flat 'It's been his job since he was two' — Ron Howard was literally a child actor, so his charm is a professional performance, not genuine warmth
jokes.explanation: A genuinely exceptional joke. 'It's been his job since he was two' is both factually true (Ron Howard was a child actor from age two) and a devastating commentary on whether celebrity warmth is authentic. The line works on every level simultaneously — biographical fact, cynical observation, and character-specific (only Rebel could say this about her father). Extremely quotable. High economy score.

jokes.text: Rebel and George Michael bonding: 'Sometimes, you want to look in your parents' perfect, sweet, kind faces, and tell them off.' / George Michael: 'Punch them.' / Rebel: 'Why would you say that?' / George Michael: 'No, no, I... That... I don't feel that way, but certain kids do.'
jokes.setup: Rebel vents about her parent's perfection
jokes.punchline: George Michael blurts 'Punch them' — revealing his actual feelings about Michael — then immediately backtracks and cites 'a website' where these feelings exist
jokes.explanation: George Michael's 'Punch them' blurt is a great character moment — his genuine feelings about Michael leaking out. The scrambled backtrack 'certain kids do... there's a website about it' is a good cover.

jokes.text: Rebel offers to loan George Michael bail money, calling it a 'bailout' — George Michael's eyes light up
jokes.setup: George Michael is about to confess Fakeblock isn't real
jokes.punchline: Rebel preemptively offers a loan — 'everybody needs a bailout now and then' — at the exact moment George Michael was about to do the right thing, making it impossible for him to come clean
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed irony: the narrator announces George Michael is about to come clean, and Rebel's offer immediately makes that impossible. The Bluth family curse of good intentions derailed by temptation lands perfectly. 'Everybody needs a bailout' as the triggering phrase — echoing the family's endless financial rescues — is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Rebel after kissing George Michael: 'It's like kissing your cousin. Ugh.' / George Michael: 'Ew. Okay. Gross, okay. Got it.'
jokes.setup: Rebel and George Michael kiss after their positive barbecue experience
jokes.punchline: Rebel uses 'like kissing your cousin' as a negative comparison — not knowing that George Michael has literally kissed his cousin and it was not, in fact, gross to him
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke. Rebel uses 'kissing your cousin' as a universal negative that everyone agrees on — not knowing that George Michael is a person who has literally kissed his cousin and still has complicated feelings about it. His 'Ew. Okay. Gross, okay. Got it' is the precise sound of someone being told their secret kink is universally disgusting. The callback to the episode's opening (Maeby proposing they make out) completes a perfect circle. Near-faultless craft: it works as a throwaway idiom, as dramatic irony, as a callback, and as character revelation simultaneously. One of the best single jokes of the season.

jokes.text: Stan tells 'Annette' (Maeby): 'I see the real you: a special, wonderful, unique, 67-year-old woman who amazes me every day. It would be my pleasure to be the man in your life that your father never was.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has been worried Stan's behavior is romantic
jokes.punchline: Stan's speech reveals his interest is entirely paternal — 'the man in your life that your father never was' — he's been trying to be a father figure to an elderly woman who is actually a teenage girl in disguise
jokes.explanation: A beautifully layered misdirection payoff. Stan's 'paternal interest' is the reveal, and it works on multiple levels: he's offering fatherly love to a 'elderly woman,' who is actually a teenage girl who very much lacks a father (Tobias), creating a dense ironic stack. The reveal is both touching and absurd. High earned vs. cheap because every layer was planted earlier in the episode.

jokes.text: Maeby (moved by Stan's paternal speech): 'Bury me.'
jokes.setup: Stan delivers a touching paternal speech to 'Annette'
jokes.punchline: Maeby's response to finally having a father figure is the elderly-character equivalent of 'kill me' — 'Bury me' — combining her teenage exhaustion/sarcasm with her character's age-appropriate idiom
jokes.explanation: Possibly the episode's single best constructed line. 'Bury me' is the elderly character's version of 'kill me' — it works as Maeby's sardonic teenage response to an earnest situation AND as in-character dialogue for an elderly woman touched by kindness. Two words that do the work of a paragraph. The economy is essentially perfect. The character integration is flawless — it could only come from someone playing an elderly character while being a teenager who needs a father figure. Deserves comparison to the highest-scoring anchors.

jokes.text: Tobias and Murphybrown in the desert: 'Is that her? Is that Lindsay?' — pointing at 'a sickly cactus with a plastic bag on it'
jokes.setup: Tobias is desperately searching for his wife Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Both Tobias and Murphybrown momentarily believe a sickly cactus with a plastic bag on it is Lindsay — the visual comparison to Lindsay is the joke
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that is also a devastating character insult. Tobias and Murphybrown both mistake a sickly cactus for Lindsay — the image of Lindsay as a 'sickly cactus with a plastic bag on it' is a perfectly specific description that implies both her physical state and her general character. Murphybrown adding 'For a second, I was like, "Is that Lindsay?"' confirms the resemblance without laboring it.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I suppose it's true. The desert makes one see what one wants to see.' / Murphybrown: 'Is that two barrel-chested, strapping men on horseback approaching us?'
jokes.setup: Tobias philosophizes about desert hallucinations after mistaking a cactus for Lindsay
jokes.punchline: Murphybrown immediately asks if he sees 'two barrel-chested, strapping men on horseback' — implying Tobias is also seeing what HE wants to see — and there actually are two men on horseback approaching
jokes.explanation: 'Barrel-chested, strapping men' as Tobias's desert vision (both real and desired) is a great character beat. The joke works on two levels: Tobias is seeing what he wants to see, AND the vision is real this time. The specific adjectives 'barrel-chested, strapping' are perfectly Tobias.

jokes.text: Tobias claims to be George Bluth on the disputed land — 'I'm the last guy you want to kick off of your la... his land... my land.' / Murphybrown: 'Our land.'
jokes.setup: Tobias decides to impersonate George Bluth to deal with the trespass situation
jokes.punchline: Tobias's mid-sentence confusion about whose land it is — 'your la... his land... my land' — and Murphybrown's completely sincere 'Our land' interruption
jokes.explanation: Tobias's inability to commit to an impersonation mid-sentence is very in character. Murphybrown's sincere 'Our land' is a nice touch that suggests he's already emotionally invested in the Bluth family identity.

jokes.text: Tobias (as George Bluth): 'Well, how about I just... use my son Michael as a signing surface? / Get up.' / Murphybrown: 'Okay.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is presented with papers to sign as 'George Bluth'
jokes.punchline: He uses Murphybrown as a literal writing surface — 'my son Michael' — both the physical absurdity and the casual casting of Murphybrown as Michael are funny
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of using a person as a signing surface while calling them 'Michael' (the actual son) is a good Tobias impersonation moment. Murphybrown's compliant 'Okay' is a perfect beat.

jokes.text: Michael crashes the Howard barbecue and finds Ron — 'Prayer hands.' Ron: 'Yeah. Listen, a lot of stuff has changed with the family.' Ron: 'Oh, has it gotten out?'
jokes.setup: Michael crashes the Howard family barbecue to discuss the movie rights
jokes.punchline: Ron's immediate 'Oh, has it gotten out?' — referring to Buster's situation — implies Imagine Entertainment is already tracking the Bluth family's legal troubles as potential content
jokes.explanation: Functional setup joke. Ron's alert interest in the Bluth family's troubles as content is a reliable running joke about his dual role as narrator/filmmaker.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'You know I'm as straight as they come, I just got a lot on my mind right now with work, slash, am I gay, slash, Buster's in jail.'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard is explaining himself to Michael at his own family barbecue
jokes.punchline: 'Work, slash, am I gay, slash, Buster's in jail' — Ron Howard casually lists a sexual identity crisis alongside a work update as bullet points
jokes.explanation: The slash-delimited list format making 'am I gay' just another agenda item alongside 'work' and 'Buster's in jail' is a great structure joke. Ron Howard delivering this to a virtual stranger at a family barbecue adds cringe value. Good economy.

jokes.text: Michael tells Ron Howard that Buster might have killed someone 'with his robot hand' — then immediately says 'it's hush-hush'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Ron Howard to drop the Bluth family movie
jokes.punchline: Michael volunteers the specific detail of a robot hand killing while claiming it's 'hush-hush' — immediately undercutting his own secrecy request
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to keep secrets while asking for secrecy is very much in character. The robot hand detail is so specific that 'hush-hush' immediately after is absurd.

jokes.text: Ron Howard on Rebel: 'She's no Bryce, but she's like a robot. Gives the exact same performance every take. Real time-saver.'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard is enthusiastically discussing the potential docuseries
jokes.punchline: He describes his own daughter as 'like a robot' who gives 'the exact same performance every take' — framing her as efficient rather than talented — a casually devastating parental assessment
jokes.explanation: An excellent dark comedy line. Ron Howard praising his daughter as 'like a robot' for 'efficiency' — 'real time-saver' — is a beautifully specific backhanded compliment that reveals a particular kind of Hollywood parent pathology. 'She's no Bryce' (his more famous daughter) sets up the slight before the robot comparison delivers it. High character integration and very quotable.

jokes.text: Ron Howard's son Reed: 'My dad knows how to read.' / Michael: 'I see why you named him Reed. He seems smart.'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard says he doesn't know how to start music; someone called 'Reed' offers to help; Michael mishears it as Ron having music read to him
jokes.punchline: Reed's indignant defense of his father's literacy, followed by Michael's immediate sycophantic compliment ('he seems smart') about a child who just said the most basic possible thing
jokes.explanation: A tight multi-layered joke: the Reed/read homophone confusion, Reed's literal-minded defense, and Michael's groveling compliment all land in quick succession. Great comic economy.

jokes.text: Ron calls for scripts — assistant says 'Streaming contracts, cable or broadcast?' / Ron: 'A streamer!' / Michael: 'No residuals.' — rapid-fire industry lingo as absurdist detail
jokes.setup: Ron Howard moves to formalize the deal immediately at the barbecue
jokes.punchline: Michael's reflexive 'No residuals' — a showbiz detail that perfectly illustrates how the Bluth family always gets the worst end of any deal
jokes.explanation: A functional industry-insider joke. Michael's 'No residuals' reads as defeat rather than objection. Works as a beat but isn't especially memorable.

jokes.text: Ron Howard's son Reed: 'Also, can you start the music?' / Ron: 'I don't know how to, Reed.' / Michael: 'I must've heard that. I must've heard that. Right? Sure. That, uh... so you get... Do you get the scripts read to you?'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard's son Reed interrupts asking if Ron can 'start the music'
jokes.punchline: Michael hears 'I don't know how to, Reed' as 'I don't know how to read' — and asks if Ron gets scripts read to him — a pun on the son's name Reed/read
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed auditory pun on Reed/read. Michael's 'I must've heard that' and careful escalation to 'Do you get scripts read to you?' is a nice commitment to the misunderstanding. The joke rewards careful listening.

jokes.text: Michael meets Reed: 'This is my son, Reed.' / Reed: 'My dad knows how to read.' / Michael: 'Yes. Nice to meet you. Uh, yeah. Whenever. I see why you named him Reed. He seems smart.'
jokes.setup: Ron introduces his son Reed after Michael's 'reading' confusion
jokes.punchline: Michael's face-saving 'I see why you named him Reed. He seems smart.' — pivoting to treating the name as a compliment about intelligence — is a perfect commitment to not admitting the mistake
jokes.explanation: Michael's graceless recovery — naming the child 'Reed' apparently because he's smart — is a good character beat. The joke completes the Reed/read wordplay arc cleanly.

jokes.text: Ron Howard: 'Hey, thanks for sneaking in.'
jokes.setup: Michael has crashed a private family barbecue at Ron Howard's house
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard thanks him for 'sneaking in' — completely normalizing the trespass with cheerful hospitality
jokes.explanation: Ron Howard's cheerful acknowledgment of Michael's trespass as something to thank him for is a great deadpan moment. Very economical — four words that say everything about Ron Howard's relentless positivity.

jokes.text: Michael runs into Bryce Howard and mistakes her for Rebel — 'I'm sorry, but I just can't, Reed.' / 'Oh, talking to your brother Reed. I get it.' / Bryce: 'I'm sorry, who are you?' / Michael: 'I'm avoiding the three-legged race.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to exit the barbecue before running into Rebel
jokes.punchline: He apologizes to 'Reed' who isn't there, then realizes he's talking to Bryce Howard who he's never met — his cover story is 'I'm avoiding the three-legged race'
jokes.explanation: The confusion compounds reasonably well. 'I'm avoiding the three-legged race' as a non-explanation is a good Michael moment — his response to social chaos is always to cite a mundane irrelevance.

jokes.text: Bryce Howard about Rebel and George Michael: 'Listen, you dodged a bullet. She's a user. She's got a new guy. And she's using him. Yeah. And I'm pretty sure it's not for his body.'
jokes.setup: Bryce tells Michael he's better off without Rebel
jokes.punchline: Bryce says Rebel is 'using' her new guy (George Michael) 'not for his body' — not knowing she's talking about Michael's son to Michael, who is now panicking
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is strong — Bryce is confirming Michael's fear about his son's girlfriend while thinking she's gossiping with an ex. 'Not for his body' is a nice dark touch.

jokes.text: Michael defending George Michael's body to Bryce: 'I don't know the guy, but I saw a picture. I think he's got a great build. You know? Um, he's wiry. You wouldn't want him to be taller. I like the young man's body. Okay? I do.'
jokes.setup: Bryce says Rebel isn't using George Michael for his body
jokes.punchline: Michael vigorously defends his son's physique to a stranger — 'I like the young man's body' — without revealing their relationship, making it sound increasingly alarming
jokes.explanation: A magnificently escalating cringe joke. Michael's defensiveness about his son's body becomes progressively more alarming without context: 'wiry,' 'you wouldn't want him to be taller,' 'I like the young man's body.' Each sentence is individually defensible as a father's pride but collectively sounds deeply wrong. The commitment to the escalation — 'Okay? I do.' — is perfect. Bryce's 'Yeah, well, that's very weird of you' is exactly right. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Bryce: 'Still, such a kiss-up. He's even dyed his hair Dallas Howard Red.' / Michael's horror / Bryce: 'But you're not supposed to leave it in that long. Look at him. Ugh. He looks like a jack-o'-lantern.'
jokes.setup: Bryce comments on Rebel's new boyfriend's hair
jokes.punchline: Michael realizes she's describing his son — 'Howard Red' — and Bryce's 'He looks like a jack-o'-lantern' is a devastating additional insult
jokes.explanation: The Howard Red callback paying off from two different angles — Ron loved it, Bryce hates it — is a good structural joke. 'Jack-o'-lantern' is a funny specific insult. Michael's horror at each new reveal works well.

jokes.text: Michael tries to exit: 'I don't want the user to create a dramatic scene.' / Bryce: 'No. Don't worry about that. I have seen her get laughs doing The Glass Menagerie.'
jokes.setup: Michael worries Rebel will make a scene
jokes.punchline: Bryce reassures him Rebel is harmless — by revealing she 'got laughs doing The Glass Menagerie,' a tragedy — implying her acting is so bad that even serious drama is accidentally comedic
jokes.explanation: A beautifully specific insult. Getting laughs during The Glass Menagerie — a Tennessee Williams tragedy — is damning. The implication is delivered as reassurance, which makes it funnier. Good economy and high originality for the specific cultural reference.

jokes.text: Bryce: 'I have seen her get laughs doing The Glass Menagerie.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he doesn't want Rebel to 'create a dramatic scene' when he leaves
jokes.punchline: Bryce's response — that she's seen Rebel get laughs doing The Glass Menagerie (a tragedy) — implies Rebel's dramatic performances are unintentionally comedic; a devastating sibling critique
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed line. The Glass Menagerie is one of the most emotionally intense American plays — getting laughs in it is a spectacular failure of dramatic acting. Bryce's delivery of this as a matter-of-fact observation about her sister, without apparent malice, is devastating. Economy is near-perfect. Justified 8+ across the board.

jokes.text: Rance Howard (Ron's elderly father): 'I thought you worked here.' / working the valet while keeping an eye on his son
jokes.setup: Michael approaches what he thinks is a valet and asks to use the camera setup to watch for his son
jokes.punchline: The 'valet' is Rance Howard — Ron Howard's real elderly father — who works the event voluntarily to keep an eye on Ron
jokes.explanation: A sweet meta moment — using Ron Howard's actual father to make a joke about fathers watching over their sons. The parallel with Michael watching for George Michael is touching and funny. Meta since Rance Howard is real.

jokes.text: Rance Howard: 'Hoax!' — about climate change, delivered cheerfully
jokes.setup: Ron Howard warns Michael that his father might share his views on climate change
jokes.punchline: Rance confirms it immediately and enthusiastically — 'Hoax!' — the single word being his entire position
jokes.explanation: The single-word delivery of 'Hoax!' is perfectly economical and the timing — Ron barely finishes warning Michael before Rance confirms — is a clean setup/punchline. Economy is essentially perfect.

jokes.text: Michael decides to go forward with the docuseries so he can 'keep an eye on the woman who might be using his son' — by putting her in the show
jokes.setup: Michael came to stop the movie/docuseries about the family
jokes.punchline: He reverses course entirely and wants to DO the docuseries — not to protect the family, but to surveil his son's girlfriend. His entire stated mission is discarded for a petty personal reason.
jokes.explanation: A great Bluth family irony. Michael's principled position (no movie) collapses the moment he has a selfish reason to reverse it. Classic Michael — dressing up personal motivation as protective parenting.

jokes.text: Ron Howard enthusiastically planning the jailhouse interview: 'Nice low-angle shot for the close-up... Oh, a wide shot. Tell it in one image.' / Michael: 'Yes, a wide shot.' / Ron: 'Wide shot.'
jokes.setup: Ron Howard gets excited about the creative possibilities of filming Buster in jail
jokes.punchline: Ron and Michael chant 'Wide shot' together in unison — a completely absurd moment of shared creative excitement that underlines how quickly Michael has been co-opted
jokes.explanation: Ron Howard's director enthusiasm is a reliable comedy beat. The chanting of 'wide shot' is absurd. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'We said no leaks, and now we're streaming over everyone?'
jokes.setup: Michael explains the Bluth story will now be a streaming series
jokes.punchline: Lucille mishears/misunderstands 'streaming series' as literally streaming/leaking — 'we said no leaks, and now we're streaming over everyone' — a technology pun delivered in outraged Lucille fashion
jokes.explanation: An excellently constructed wordplay joke that is also deeply character-specific. Lucille's technological ignorance combined with her outrage produces a perfect malapropism that accidentally makes sense. 'Streaming over everyone' is the right kind of wrong. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'They'll drop the charges quicker than you can blink your...' / Michael: 'Initiate, at least, the blinking of the eyes. The opening of the eyes is, uh, a bit of a guestimate lately.'
jokes.setup: Lucille sets up an idiom about 'quicker than you can blink your eyes'
jokes.punchline: Michael interrupts to clarify that Lucille can't actually blink properly — 'the opening of the eyes is a bit of a guestimate lately' — referencing her eye surgery running gag
jokes.explanation: 'The opening of the eyes is a bit of a guestimate lately' is a great line — specific, absurd, and perfectly in keeping with Lucille's vanity complications. Good callback execution.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'And what if Buster chokes?' / Michael: 'I guess that's what Ron's for.' / Narrator: 'That would prove to be a very... No, you'll see on the next "On the Next."'
jokes.setup: Michael explains why the Ron Howard interview will help Buster
jokes.punchline: The narrator begins a foreshadowing sentence — 'That would prove to be a very...' — then catches itself and redirects to the 'On the Next' segment — a self-aware structural joke about the show's own format
jokes.explanation: A clever meta-structural joke — the narrator self-corrects and kicks the resolution to the 'On the Next' segment, drawing attention to the show's own format as a comedic device. The 'no, you'll see on the next On the Next' is a nice self-aware button.

jokes.text: 'On the Next' segment: Ron Howard interviews Buster in jail — Buster remembers George Sr.'s advice and charges at a 'skinhead' who is actually the camera operator
jokes.setup: George Sr. advised Buster to charge at skinheads for respect — Buster immediately misidentified a guard
jokes.punchline: In jail, Buster sees a 'skinhead' — presumably a bald camera operator — and charges at him during the Ron Howard interview, with Ron calmly saying 'Make a note. Forget the wide shots.'
jokes.explanation: A triumphant multi-layered callback payoff. The skinhead advice, Buster's inability to identify who is and isn't a skinhead, Ron Howard's 'wide shot' obsession — all three threads converge in the 'On the Next.' Ron's calm director response 'Make a note. Forget the wide shots. 50/50 and matching overs.' to Buster attacking someone is exceptional deadpan. Buster's 'See what I did to skinhead?' is perfectly in character. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: 'On the Next': Maeby and Stan looking at a photo of themselves together — Stan: 'Look how happy they look together. Just like us.' / Maeby: 'Until this happens.' — ending on a freeze frame of apparent disaster
jokes.setup: The new paternal relationship between Stan and 'Annette' seems warm and genuine
jokes.punchline: Maeby's ominous 'Until this happens' cut to black — suggesting the relationship will be complicated by some future reveal, likely involving her actual identity
jokes.explanation: A functional teaser joke. Works as a structural button on the episode more than as a standalone comedic moment. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing what 'this happens' likely refers to.

tmdb_overview: Michael attempts to bail out Buster. George-Michael finally meets the Howards, and Ron informs Michael that his plans for the movie have changed.
insight: Steady character comedy sustains a 78-point episode despite minimal standout moments.
